Hello and welcome everybody for another great call live call with Dr. Robert Chaldeany fantastic you're all here still so see some people are joining us great to see I see hey Justin good to see you Flavio nice to see you in a rash as you all see I'm getting better each day I want to thank everybody for their very kind supporting messages as you heard the first thought was from a team that there were some technical issues because I was in the hospital with food poisoning so I couldn't reach out but thank you very much for your kind messages and supporting messages I really appreciate it and we got our energy back so that's good because we are here in a great call with Dr. Chaldeany one thing that I like to share before we start is of course in the upcoming week we have two live calls which I will host especially I want to invite everybody to join on Saturday I will deeply explain the business model but also that are the exact models when I became in 2017 a Chaldeany certified trainer I needed to go out there I wasn't in the exact same position as you you know about these principles you had your education now how to build a business with it so I will share everything I done to make for me this is a successful journey and I I ended up being a partner with Dr. Chaldeany himself so I think that could be very successful if you join that call and and learn from that and also it gives you a much better and clearer understanding how you can bring the knowledge and the size of influence to a bigger audience which is one of our missions it's the mission at the Chaldeany Institute so we are two minutes over the top of the hour so let's start and as always I like to start with asking some questions that give you some deeper insight in Dr. Chaldeany and his journey in behavioral science so Dr. Chaldeany very much a big welcome again and thank you for joining us great to see you I'm looking forward to it I always do thank you as yes Jason I need to go to a barber as well there was also scheduled last weekend I see that comment in the chat fantastic Dr. Chaldeany that you are here and as always we like to start with I like to start with asking you some questions before everybody gets the opportunity to ask you a question the first question I like to ask you today we all know you were a professor at Arizona State University why and how did you choose Arizona State University well it is a good question because at the time that I first came I had offers from other places I was in I was finishing up my postdoctoral training at Columbia University in New York City I had an offer to stay in New York I had a couple of other offers one at a at the University of Toronto a great school and then I had a visit to Arizona State and what I realized is that those other schools were very traditional and and already settled in the kinds of ways that they were going to approach education and research but ASU was just beginning and you could feel the energy here there was a burgeoning sense of a progression and advancement that was a part of the university because it was so new and the state of Arizona was was also new and this university was was relatively known it was only be it only became a university in 1957 and you could feel that there was also an increase in population to the southwest and especially Arizona and Phoenix we were part of what was called the Sun Belt everybody moving from the east and north and Midwest and so on into these places and I just thought that that energy fit where I was a young startup assistant professor looking to harness that energy and and move forward with it and the university was very willing to support its young people rather than just giving all of its resources to its full professors and its established faculty they wanted to build their reputation based on the the work of the people who were coming in and eager to move forward I was one of those people and they were very willing to be supportive of my research provide me with the resources that I needed and so that plus the fact that there were palm trees in my backyard if I came here and I'm going up in Wisconsin that I the only time I'd ever seen a palm tree was on the the tournament of roses parade on thanks on New Year's Day that you would see happening before the Rose Bowl and and that was it that was my exposure but then I had an opportunity to be in the place that had swimming pools and palm trees in their backyard so that was a factor not the biggest one oh fantastic love this story yeah and what a great journey it's been setting up the and giving credits there because at this moment it's a well-established university and with a great faculty on psychology well I'll tell you something I always expected well this would be my first job because in academic in academia one moves up by moving on to the next university the next opportunity the next recruitment where they try to sweep you away to to come and work with them so I always thought that I would move on but I I have made a number of decisions to stay sometimes decisions were so difficult that I broke out in a rash all over my back but I still decided to stay because the place has come from to a place of a truly first-rate university because of the leadership of the last two presidents of the university and it really gave me confidence in the idea that a leader can make a big difference from moving an organization in the right direction if that leader holds the the proper values and has the determination and skill to do so it really does make a big difference so that's another factor in deciding to stay. Fantastic and one of the characteristics about you Dr. Chaldini that I love and I think everybody does it's your humbleness so that's why I'm going to make this statement I don't know if you know but at Arizona State University is the Dr. Robert Chaldini behavioral lab where they conduct scientific behavioral research so a lab is named after Dr. Chaldini so fantastic let me jump into the second question I'm very curious about this answer what do three buckets of water have to do with influence? So when I was first teaching courses in persuasion social influence attitude change and so on I wanted to make evident in a very hands-on way the idea of perceptual contrast we talk about it in all our materials that if someone is exposed to a certain kind of level of experience with one item let's say the weight so if you first lift something heavy and then lift something light the second one seems lighter than it actually is in contrast to the first and it works the other way if you first lift a paperclip and then asked or asked to lift a heavy book I have one right here as an example the second one seems heavier than if I hadn't first asked them to lift it so this works in all forms of human behavior and I wanted to make that point in the fact that for example when you present let's say a price for your product or the productivity of your organization begin with an example of a larger number or price or set of and and or excuse me begin yeah if you want your price to seem reasonable begin with a larger one and then tell them what your price is start by anchoring them with a a number that's relevant right to the to the issue and makes your price looks better look better for example anyway the way I used three buckets of water to make this point was when I would teach the class I would ask a student to come to the front of the room and sit in front of three pails of water one was hot one was cold and the one in the middle was room temperature right and I would ask the student to put a hand in each of the two extreme buckets of water right and keep them there for about 10 or 15 seconds and then plunge plunge both simultaneously in the middle bucket of room temperature water and you would see the same look of astonishment on students faces every time because the hand that was in the hot water now felt like it was in cool water and the hand that was in the cold water now felt like it was in warm water it was the same bucket of water right but it what the experience of it depended entirely on what they had experienced before right and there is a great lesson for us when it comes to the influence process what do we ask people to experience before we present our offer or product or service greatly influences how they experience that offer or product or service what a fantastic story I love that how you may get come to life the contrast phenomenon and as Chaldeany certified professionals what an amazing example so if you are ever with a group take three buckets of water to show them real life how powerful the contrast phenomenon is I'll tell you what later on when I would do this when we started to have advances in technology the people who would put their hands and one hand would feel different from the the other hand in the same water they would look under the bucket to see if we had some sort of cooling and heating system on each side no no it was the phenomenon of perceptual contrast fantastic analogy to make that happen I love this example thanks for sharing I'll ask one more question and of course then all you guys can ask questions to Dr. Chaldeany I think this question a lot of people are looking to get the answer to this question why did you add the seventh principle of unity yes so I I think it had to do with a couple of factors one was I started seeing the research the literature in behavioral science showing that people who shared a sense of personal or social identity they they have the same political party affiliation or religious affiliation or locality or whatever that might be all right that made them feel a sense of weeness to those who shared that category right right became much more willing to say yes and to follow those individuals who were there in that we like category right they would say yes to them they there was there was greater attitude change that you could get there was greater trust there was greater loyalty there was greater willingness to forgive mistakes right you just gave like you you gave grace to the people who were in this category with you right so if it was possible to uh so so that seemed to me a form of influence that I hadn't really talked about with the other principles of influence the closest thing were the like the the liking principle in which you would say if somebody is similar to you you like that person more that's true but this isn't about liking it's about belonging it's about the importance of feeling a belonging connection to someone else this person is not just if I were to say to everybody else you know you know boss he's like us that would be good but if I said boss is one of us that's an entirely different and elevated level of sympathy and willingness to work with boss to follow boss to to support boss and so on right so that was what I started to recognize in the literature and then there were two studies I uh that had almost stayed in my mind there are studies that I know I will encounter these studies and they won't leave me alone and one of them was like that the other one was a study that my my research team did uh earlier before I started thinking about the the process of of unity it was under what circumstances will people help somebody else who needs their help but they don't they're not required to help at all they would have to do something to sacrifice their time or energy or even the quality of their experience to help this other person and the research is part of a long tradition that shows that well if you empathize with that person if you put yourself in that person's situation you become much more helpful and the reason that was offered by principally a researcher named daniel battson at university of kansas was when you empathize you feel a greater sense of compassion for that person all right that person's need you want to you want to help that person because you feel sympathy or compassion with their situation because you've put yourself in that person's situation and therefore you recognize how dire it might be all right so that was the existing view but we thought there might be another reason when you empathize empathize with another person you actually put yourself in that person in other words you that person becomes of you you become of them you cross the borders between separate identities and you share an identity with that person when you've empathized with them and put yourself in their shoes so we did a series of research to examine what this was and it was very clear it was not perceptions of of of compassion that led to altruism led to helping it was perceptions of connection the extent to which people felt that they shared some kind of identity with this person after having empathized with them that was the difference so that was one study right that led me think well it's not what we usually think it is it's this other thing this sense of of connection all right the second one was a study not about influence in a business situation or even a helping situation it was a with a stranger it was how influence works inside existing relationships there's a study done in in texas and researchers took couples existing romantically connected couples who had been together for at least two years and they brought them into the laboratory and they said okay we're going to do an experiment we want you to come upon or tell us about a difference between you a difference in opinion something where you just have not been able to find a way to reconcile this difference of opinion it may have to do with who takes out the garbage or whatever it is something probably more deep than that but one what's something that you just as a couple have not been able to resolve and then we want one of you to be the persuader and try to convince your partner to come into line with you in your position right okay and all right so where they would flip a coin one of them would become the persuader the other would be the recipient of the persuasive message and then the researchers left the room but they didn't really leave the room they had tape recorders they were watching through one way mirrors they could see what was going on and they found three kinds of persuaders one they call the coercive punishing persuader this is the one who said look if you don't do this you'll be sorry i'm going to have to do something that you won't like not only did that fail to be successful it actually polarized the two the person who received that kind of coercive punishing thing moved further away from their partner then there was a second kind of communicator that the researchers call the logical reasonable communicator they would say to their partner look if you'll just take a closer examination of the situation that we're in you'll see that i'm right you'll see that you you have the the the wrong position on this well that didn't produce polarization it just produced laughter from they just dismissed this i know yeah you think you know what's right so no no movement at all and then there was the third group and it was only about 15 percent of them who were smart enough to use the principle of unity here's what they would say you know we've been together now for four years i really wish you would do this one that's all that wasn't there was no new empirical evidence there wasn't anything having to do with logic it was that we are a couple we share a relationship but an identity as a couple they brought that to consciousness and all of those things that i mentioned earlier that go with a a we relationship willingness to change loyalty affection various kinds of positive features now they came to the surface and dominated the choice that second that third group was was the only one that produced significant movement because they simply brought the concept of weaness to the surface and there are certain accompaniments with weaness that come automatically right now it turns out that those people most of them said this sort of thing well we've been together now for three years or whatever i really wish you could do but there were two other kinds of unity messages one said you know we care for each other we love each other i really wish you would do this for wow and that got an effect and the simplest one was to use the pronouns we our and us in describing the situation bringing the connection to the surface right rather than the difference between the remember what the researchers did was to say find something that separates you find something that divides you right this 15 percent would not stand for it they recognize that there was something more transcendent than separation there was unity and they brought attention to it and then the relationship was the message you didn't have to change the logic or the empirical evidence for your message the relationship the existence of the relationship became the message and so i knew i needed to break this out and study it and and report it in my uh in the newest version of the influence book what an amazing story dr chaldeely thanks for sharing i know one thing for everybody that currently is in a relationship in this call this call is proven its value already well thanks thanks for sharing and it's also time of course for everybody to be able to ask a question to you dr chaldeely for people who are joined first here my name is boss wow does i'm the CEO of the chaldeely institute you can raise your hand by go to reaction and there is a button raise hand you will get in the queue and we will handle as much questions as possible so fantastic thanks for sharing again dr chaldeely and i see my friend matt champagne is at the front of the queue so matt thanks for joining i'd love to see you again and i would say the floor is yours hello matt terrific thanks boss thanks dr chaldeely a question about your influence book i've used your story to the best of my recollection i tell the story to students as an example of persistence i don't know if my story is spot on but i recall you saying there's about 1980s-ish late 80s you're speaking at Purdue and influence had been out for a while but i think you had said nobody's read it it had been out for quite a while and we were all required to read journal articles we didn't know about influence so i use that to my students as an example of persistence even though you know the idea is right and you've done this work it sometimes takes years for it to catch fire and i wondered was there a spark like what made it go from sort of unrecognized to now eight million people having the book was it a certain person or a certain event that that kicked that off it was two things one happened rather precipitously and the other happened gradually the first was that around the time that the book was first published most in most advice as to how to change the influence process how to get people to buy things how to get managers to be more successful in getting their their their co-workers on board with new initiatives and so on came from stories they would tell anecdotes examples from their history or times that they could reflect and give illustrations of how this worked in the past and then after not long after that about two or three years later everybody started to believe in the idea of evidence-based decision-making it became the trend it became the go-to-gold standard for how decision makers should choose make their choices right and the book influence was this handy place for a certain kind of influence information information about how you influence people that was based on scientific evidence one particular kind of evidence and there it was all in one place and the book became kind of a poster child for that sort of approach before you make a decision be sure you understand how to influence people to follow that decision right that was one thing this change in the times and it happened very precipitously the other was although the book had not sold many copies in a general audience it was being assigned in seminars especially in business schools on management and and marketing right and the and the it was MBAs for the most part they were being asked to read this book they then left their MBA programs and went into business organizations and in a year or two became the the manager of the western division right of sales or whatever it was and they started buying the book for each of their people the people who were under them and then those people who benefited from the insights that were there when they moved on and moved up they would buy the book for all of their personnel and that started the the snowball but it wasn't that one wasn't so precipitous that one was a kind of a snowballing effect and then by at a certain point there was a there was a tipping point where people started talking about it and and employing it I recognize when that happened because people started pronouncing my name correctly I suddenly recognized that I wasn't being called Dr. Cialdini anymore they were actually they knew my name because the book had become somewhat of a of a bestseller as a result yeah thank you I was always curious thanks yeah great question and thanks for that great answer Dr. Cialdini last time I made a promise that some people were in the front of the queue and one of them was Michelle Caprio so I like to give her the word first because I told and promised that she could jump the queue in this session so Michelle the floor is yours thank you very much Dr. Cialdini I would like for you to help me with an influence fail my fail I'm sure I was trying to leverage the principle of reciprocity in my business and what I desire to do is I need online reviews of my business I'm finding that's increasingly more valuable in my area as a consultant so the first time I tried reciprocity I this was before I got too far into your your influence material I would ask them for a review and if they did I would send them a $10 star book I would say if if you'll help me out and partner with me in making sure my business flourishes I would really appreciate it if you can help me with this I'll send you a $10 star book okay I got about maybe 10% response so then I was trying to leverage what you had mentioned that the McDonald's study where they actually offered something and then that created the rest the feeling of reciprocity and so then I tried sending them out sending them their receipt for the services and then including a $10 star books copy gift card no no conditions uh no strings but I did ask them would you please help me uh in this endeavor and I got like zero zero and so I I know I have misapplied that principle and I would like for you to I'm just telling the whole group that I did it wrong so um can you debrief me on that please so I can you didn't do it wrong Michelle it's just that the what you were asking them to do and paying them for it sounded like a bribe to write something positive about you and I think people felt that uh resistant to being given this $10 in with the expectation that you were going to influence them to write something positive I think that was if I were in that position and I received the $10 I was oh come on you can't buy me you can't buy my my uh my evaluation or just my evaluation before this right so uh yeah I think that was uh that's I think I would instead of using reciprocity I'd use uh testimonials from other people who had done your your your um received your your services right and said good had positive things to say and just say to people and show that we've got an increasing a trend of more and more people who have said good things about us would you join those people if you feel the same way would you join those people to do this and and now you have essentially the social proof principle working for you right well so I didn't I must have not I I they they had already had my services and I was seeing them a follow-up letter and then ask the same thing yeah same thing so if you they've used can show yeah show them the trend of people who have reviewed your services positively okay and ask them to join those individuals okay give the use social proof okay essentially now here's as long as we're on the the this topic of reviews those are they're very important these days product reviews services reviews star ratings and so on and the question is what do you do with somebody who writes a negative review and gives you one star or two stars has some sort of complaint about you but the majority of people are you you're up at four and five stars is that does that kept capture your situation michelle yes yeah okay what do you do then well first of all you do respond you do say I'm sorry that you had this experience and if there's a way for you to to make an adjustment you say that we certainly will make an adjustment but you say we're sorry you had this experience and then say but we're so glad that your experience does not match the great majority of the people so see what you've done there you move them away from the negative onto the social proof of all the positive that's how you handle a negative review move people away from that thought that that thought that negativity to the positivity that you have in your back pocket that it's genuinely there the key is to shift focus okay this is powerful and michelle I want to thank you that you were so transparent and open and I think a lot of people learned a lot from your question and thanks dr chaldeany for sharing that lot with me because I think everybody from us everybody in the world needs to deal with reviews and how to deal with a negative review wow that was powerful advice michelle thanks again for your great and openness thank you freddy can by the way we by the way we just got one we just got one for the book it was uh it was you know this book was way too long right and so they gave it like two stars but right below that was one I would reread this book right so so watch again provide the negative one and then show that it doesn't match what everybody else is saying and now you've got the you've harnessed the power of social proof well thanks and that's a lovely real-life example dr chaldeany thank you for sharing and the other thing about it is once again you certify yourself as being transparent and being willing to talk about a weakness so that what you say next gets imbued with the credibility that goes with being an honest broker of information about yourself thanks yes and which leads to authority to trustworthiness trustworthiness yep fantastic freddy let me give you the floor hello hello bas how are you hello dr chaldeany how are you ready dr chaldeany for me this moment is an honor uh I am a great admirer of your work and I just want to tell you that I like you very much and and I'm happy to be talking to you right now I wanted to ask you about concessions um I I can recall um that you suggested that the number of concessions that um we it's best for us to offer when when we give a proposal to a client is two additional concessions so in total would be three concessions do we do we have any additional science-based argument to back up why it has to be two additional uh no more than that it's two it's two total concessions you begin with an initial position and one concession from it and the other one after that but the concessions should be should be accompanied by lower prices that is you you are not don't just say all right we'll give you the same thing for less money because then they say to themselves well wait a minute why did they offer me this larger price in the first place were they trying to to extract uh unreasonable undo uh profits from me so always the concession should not be okay we'll give you this product for less money it is okay but we if you're not interested in the platinum package here's the gold package all right we can give you this all right and if you're not interested in that here's the silver package right so in in each case you have more you you have a couple of additional options besides the the largest uh or the the most high quality option you should offer but you should always offer the highest quality presentation yes first so the so that the price of the others will seem lower when you come to them after anchoring them on that higher price right i just saw an article that showed that they did this across a whole range of different uh products and services if you give if you give a list of uh opportunities at different prices if you begin with the highest price they spend significantly more money than if you begin with the lowest price and move up okay so by concessions i'm not talking about giving away value i'm talking about moving your package to one that fits with their needs or budget that's that's what i mean by concession concession thank you very much thank you very question freddy thank you boss thank you so next i see faiz faiz welcome yes thank you uh dr chalini and bas waltz this is faiz from malaysia faiz your opinion doctor on no orientated questions does that sound manipulative in sales uh there is this technique called no orientated question where we ask questions to have the other party to say no to that and we take it as a yes commitment um for example uh would you oppose the idea of scheduling a demo next week and they say no and then we take that as a yes for a commitment does that sound manipulative i don't understand why that would be a yes unless you're saying oh well if you can't do it next week could we send you some information uh about this that that would be uh i if you say no to something you can say well how about this uh that seems fine to me you begin with what you would most like or what you think would most uh be informative for these individuals but if they say no how i don't see how that's a commitment to your to your product unless you offer them another version of what you're suggesting that would be less time consuming i would say yeah because according to this technique they're trying to uh some sort of uh driving they don't want the the prospect to say yes because they don't want them to feel committed to it so they want to drive them into because telling a no is more secure so they they reverse the question so that the prospect will say no to that and they take it as a yes so i think it's manipulative yeah i don't i don't understand the rationale because what i've always heard is uh getting people to just say yes to small things so uh if you're selling a car so would you prefer the red car to the other colors yes well would you prefer uh you know financing it through us or through your own bank could we could we finance it then you get yes and you get a series of yeses right that leads to a final yes that's the one i've always heard uh so i'm surprised at this one and i wouldn't support it yeah thank you doctor yeah great question for you and that's also there's a lot of sales training you see this technique ask questions where people just say yes and then you ask your proposal and then you're supposed to get a yes well from my own experience i want to add i don't support this and i don't think it really works if you ask somebody does the sunshine outside yes do you have a guy yes will you sign this deal well maybe no that's not a commitment right okay thank you jackson are you with us hello good to see you jackson hi jackson good to see you doctor chauh dini from delos texas i have a youtube video question the influence at work youtube channel has a science of persuasion youtube video that been using it for the past few years to educate people about the principles of persuasion that youtube video was developed created 10 years ago now with 13 million views any plans or thoughts to do an update on that video to include the principle of unity yes there are those plans because the principle of unity as i discussed at the very outset is central you know here's the mistake i made in the past when i thought about the principle of unity i always thought about it as a as a amplifier of the others that if you have a sense of community with somebody a sense of existing relationship that will make all the other principles work better it's just an accelerant no it belongs independent of those other it would work all by itself so it does it does warrant a change in that it's it's essentially an animation if i if i i know which one you're talking about and yeah it's had over 13 million views people use it in classrooms and so on it's very effective so as a as a mechanism of getting the the general ideas across to people it's something that's worthy of adjusting for unity but there's an interesting conflict in sending out something like this does it undercut our larger program that is if people get the principles of influence why do they need us why do they and the key is you have to give them an honest sampling of what they will get within the program and promise them much more promise them accurately that they will get much more than this if they like this there's more of it to be had inside the the practitioners program yeah i have a follow-up question about the practice program in your book influence new and expanded under the liking chapter you identify the four summaries or activators including physical attractiveness similarity compliments and cooperation however in the practitioner program under the chapter of liking you identify the activators as similarity praise and cooperation you deliberately left out physical attractiveness in the practitioner program i was wondering the reason why you choose to do that you know that's the hardest thing to change about ourselves right so but it's one of the things that it doesn't strike me as it's as something that lends itself to to the merits of what you're presenting it's something superficial right but uh so uh i think that's why we decided that isn't you know we can give people advice uh get your hair cut you know uh make sure that it but that doesn't that doesn't merit a choice on the basis of the merits of the case right so i think that's why we decided against it i think other things like mentioning true similarities does work because if you recognize that the person that you're dealing with if you if you find something that's similar between the two of you that person will like you more right and you will like that person more and now you have two people who like each other doing business with one another and it doesn't get any better than that it you've created a better experience by highlighting the similarities because that you're as human as they are and you will experience liking for that person making it more likely that you will do whatever you can to be sure that that person's experience is a positive one in the exchange yeah thanks dr chalbini great question uh jackson two great questions and thank for the fantastic answer i love this subtle nuance what you explained at the end well i've always said that in in all the sales training programs that i've ever experienced we were always told that getting your customer to like you is the number one rule of sales i think it's an important rule but it's not the number one rule the number one rule is coming to like your customer and showing that when they see that you like them they exhale a breath they say phew i'm in the hands of an expert who likes me this is where i want to be with this person who likes me right rather than oh i like that person because oh jackson's funny or he's good looking or you know no that that doesn't enhance the the the outcomes for that person but if you've come to like them their outcomes will be better because you'll see to it that their experience is is elevated is is upgraded fantastic thanks a lot dr chalbini as we all see we have eight minutes left and i keep my promises like you saw with michelle so next time i will open the queue with oil that's still on raising the hand we quickly continue to patrick hello patrick live from australia today uh no not yet bus next week i'll be back in australia brought you a small sample of the uh key lime pie where whatever it was and that would be enough to to cause you to get the the whole slice of the pie that's i think what we're talking about giving people tastes of what they will be able to receive giving them samples of what they will be able to learn at a much deeper and broader level if they move in the direction of the of the the entire compliance driven um and so you talked a little bit about um the three types of influencers or persuaders and one of them is the coercive um persuader if you don't do this it'll be bad for you that is a reality in the industry i mean we try not to draw that line in the sand but i'm curious about your your thoughts on um and i'll leave it with us with you you might need to spend some time on it but there is literature that is providing support that the positive interaction with the the claim agent as in providing good customer service um being nice being friendly treating them with dignity does not impact on the return to work of a person who's off work and that duration return to work is the outcome and so they're not tracking well together which means that people who spend all their time building these relationships trying to create these positive experiences wind up having to draw this line in the sand because it's like okay the claim's coming to an end and what they're feeling is that all that effort to to be influential in those relationship pieces is undone because now all that likeability i spend all that time creating likeability in relationship it's all undone when i gotta say no i'm just curious on a very high level just your thought on that insight and then maybe how to think about approaching to get better outcomes well the closest one i have i'm not sure the context uh that you're working within jason applies directly but in the uk uh there there was a unit that had to find work for people who were who's who were on the dole they weren't working they were getting uh they were getting uh government uh supplements and so on and these individuals uh in the unit would set up meetings for uh the unemployed with potential employers right and what they found was even though they had positive relationships with the uh uh with these individuals they often didn't go in and show up at the meetings but if they did one thing different that based on the rule of reciprocity they significantly increased the number of unemployed people who would actually go and have a meeting it was to say honestly instead of saying so i've arranged a meeting with you with x y z corporation on thursday at three o'clock they said here's what i did in order to get that meeting i called jack over at uh the corporation he uh worked with janet we worked together to find a free time that would be available for them all right and we'd like you to to be at that meeting simply telling people how much effort they went through rather than just how much you like me what did i do in order to produce this possibility for you significantly increase the number of people who did it so show your work if you've done a lot of effort in in behalf of a particular individual then the rule of reciprocity says well now it's my turn to do something in return that's the closest i think i can come to the setting it may not be exactly the one that you that you described jason but it but uh that's the one i know some research about so that's helpful um on you know in terms of that so like i said it's a really big question it's a really big thing that we struggle in the industry so
